title: Direct Pulp Capping: When to Attempt Vital Pulp Therapy
excerpt: Understanding the clinical indicators that determine whether a pulp exposure can be managed conservatively.
body:
When a pulp exposure occurs during caries excavation, the decision to attempt direct pulp capping versus proceeding to pulpotomy or root canal therapy depends on several clinical and patient factors.

## Favorable indicators

Direct pulp capping has the highest success rate when the exposure is small (less than 1 mm), occurs in a field with good hemorrhage control, and involves a tooth with no prior symptoms of irreversible pulpitis. The patient's age matters: younger teeth with open apices and robust blood supply respond more favorably.

## Material selection

Calcium silicate cements such as MTA or Biodentine have largely replaced traditional calcium hydroxide for direct pulp capping. These materials offer better sealing ability, biocompatibility, and long-term bridge formation. Place the material directly over the exposure site without excessive pressure.

## Unfavorable scenarios

- Spontaneous or lingering pain before the procedure
- Exposure larger than 2 mm with uncontrollable hemorrhage
- Radiographic signs of periapical pathology
- Carious pulp exposure in a mature tooth with a long history of deep caries

## Follow-up protocol

Pulp vitality testing at 1 week, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year post-treatment helps detect early failure. A tooth that develops spontaneous pain, sensitivity to heat, or periapical radiolucency should be scheduled for root canal therapy.

## Clinical takeaway

Not every pulp exposure requires endodontic treatment. Careful case selection and proper material choice give clinicians a predictable conservative option that preserves tooth vitality.

author.name: Dr. Marcus Rivera
author.institution: Columbia University College of Dental Medicine
